moment_text: One another thing I've really become interested in is internal family systems, which it basically speaks to the fact that we all are made up of. Parts. Some of those parts are amplified and valued, like with me, the talker, the entertainer, and some parts are exiled because they don't serve the family system or nobody wants to hear it. And what happens to those exiled parts is they keep getting louder and louder. And then the other part of, um, internal family systems that I find really interesting is to keep those parts at bay. There are, there are managers that come in. So there are two types of managers. There's ones that are like the overfunctioners, the super organized, the people pleasers, I'm raising my hand. All of those things. And those are very socially accepted, but they're still keeping the parts of us that we don't wanna deal with at bay. And then they're the firefighters. And the firefighters just as it sounds like they come out and they immediately, they're not managing, they're putting out the fire. So that's addiction. You know, anger, you know, more, uh, uh, more, uh, less desirable. Social behaviors, right? So how can we integrate all of those parts of us? They're, they all have value. They were exiled for a reason, but they play a role. Or maybe they played a role for us when we were a child and we really needed them.
